implemented: Added 7 structured (receiver, field) member-access entries to ELIXIR_AST_SOURCES (4: IO.gets, System.get_env, File.read, File.read!) and ELIXIR_AST_SINKS (3: System.cmd, Code.eval_string, Ecto.Adapters.SQL.query) in crates/tldr-core/src/security/taint.rs alongside the existing raw-substring ("", "X.y") duplicates. Multi-segment receiver Ecto.Adapters.SQL handled via the W2-pre call-shape path (extract_call_name_elixir + rfind('.') split keeps the full dotted prefix as receiver). Updated the partial-coverage comment at L2806-2815 to note Module.function shapes are now structurally matched and multi-segment receivers are supported. Mirrored M2's in-place extension approach: appended structured tuples to existing member_patterns arrays — preserves struct count, minimizes diff, keeps source_type/sink_type grouping logically clean. Sanitizer banks (ELIXIR_AST_SANITIZERS) untouched. ELIXIR_PATTERNS regex bank untouched (M5 deletes). Per M1 architectural finding (member_patterns_match raw-substring fallback at taint.rs:3088-3092 catches Module.function shapes under analyze_ast_only via descendant_text.contains()), the structured entries are functionally additive in W2-pre — they will become load-bearing when M5 deletes both the regex banks AND the raw-substring duplicates atomically. No behavior change at the test level; M3 is preparatory.
harness_mechanism: N/A for M3 — uses the analyze_ast_only thread-local harness added in M1.

tests.rr_module_function_integ_test_summary: 21 total: 18 PASSED, 3 FAILED. The 7 Elixir Module.function fixtures on the regular path (analyze_with_ssa) ALL PASS: elixir_io_gets_to_system_cmd, elixir_system_get_env_to_code_eval, elixir_file_read_to_system_cmd, elixir_file_read_bang_to_system_cmd, elixir_user_input_to_code_eval_string, elixir_user_input_to_ecto_sql_query, elixir_pipe_operator_io_gets_to_system_cmd. The 3 FAILED tests are identical to the M2 RED state (UNCHANGED): (a) ruby_io_popen_with_user_input_via_compute_taint AST-only path — bare `gets` parses as identifier not call_expression in Ruby tree-sitter; pre-existing bare-call AST coverage gap (M1 architectural_finding_2); (b) elixir_string_literal_system_cmd_substring_zero_findings REGULAR path — pre-existing string-literal regex/AST raw-fallback false positive (M1 architectural_finding_3); (c) ocaml_string_literal_sys_command_substring_zero_findings REGULAR path — pre-existing OCaml regex string-leak (M1 architectural_finding_3). All 3 FAILs are orthogonal to the M3 Module.function-shape rewrites and will resolve at M5 (when raw-fallback duplicates AND regex banks are deleted, structured-shape match correctly skips string contexts).

files_per_change.purpose: Added 7 structured-shape (receiver, field) tuples to ELIXIR_AST_SOURCES (4: IO.gets, System.get_env, File.read, File.read!) and ELIXIR_AST_SINKS (3: System.cmd, Code.eval_string, Ecto.Adapters.SQL.query) alongside existing raw-fallback duplicates; updated partial-coverage comment to reflect new structural matching path and multi-segment receiver support.
Full cargo test capture for audit trail.

loc_delta_actual.vs_contract_estimate: Contract estimated ~21 LOC; actual 13 net (21 insertions, 8 deletions). Aligned with M2's experience: existing entries already had multi-element member_patterns arrays for File.* — extending in-place was cheaper than expected. No semantic difference vs contract.

discoveries.title: M2 finding confirmed under M3: structured additions are functionally additive — no test transition observed
discoveries.details: M2 confirmed that structured (Module, fn) rewrites are functionally additive because member_patterns_match (taint.rs:3088-3092) has a raw-substring fallback that catches `("", "X.y")` entries via descendant_text.contains() under both regular and AST-only dispatch. Empirically re-confirmed at M3: the 21-test rr_module_function_integ_test breakdown is IDENTICAL to M2 (18 PASS / 3 FAIL, same 3 FAILs). The structured entries are now present and exercised on the structural path (verified by cargo check passing — match arms compile against the existing AstSourcePattern/AstSinkPattern shape) but provide no net new gating until M5 deletes the raw-fallback duplicates atomically.
discoveries.implication_for_m4_m5: M4 (OCaml) should follow the same additive pattern — add structured entries alongside existing raw-fallback duplicates, expect zero test transitions, and confirm cargo check + no-new-regressions only. M5 ATOMIC remains the only milestone where structured entries become load-bearing.

discoveries.title: Multi-segment receiver Ecto.Adapters.SQL.query encoded successfully as ('Ecto.Adapters.SQL', 'query')
discoveries.details: Per the contract's note about Elixir's `Ecto.Adapters.SQL.query(...)` parsing — extract_call_name_elixir returns the full dotted name and the W2-pre call-shape path uses rfind('.') to split into (receiver, field). The structured tuple ('Ecto.Adapters.SQL', 'query') is the correct receiver field — does NOT need to be broken into nested tuples. cargo check passes confirming the tuple compiles against the existing AstSinkPattern shape. The corresponding Elixir test elixir_user_input_to_ecto_sql_query_via_compute_taint PASSES on the regular path via the existing raw-substring fallback ('', 'Ecto.Adapters.SQL.query'); the structured tuple is preparatory for M5 when the fallback is removed.
discoveries.implication_for_m5: When M5 deletes the raw-fallback duplicates, the structured ('Ecto.Adapters.SQL', 'query') entry must successfully match the call-shape path's rfind('.')-split output. Spot-check during M5 verification.

discoveries.title: File.read! bang-suffix function name compiles cleanly as a Rust string literal — no escaping required
discoveries.details: Initial concern (raised in the M3 contract): Elixir's `File.read!` (with the `!` convention suffix) might require special escaping in the AstSourcePattern field-name string. Empirically confirmed: cargo check passes with both ('File', 'read!') and ('', 'File.read!') as plain Rust string literals. Tree-sitter-elixir produces the bang as part of the function-name token, and rfind('.') split correctly yields ('File', 'read!') from 'File.read!' — no special handling needed. Test elixir_file_read_bang_to_system_cmd_via_compute_taint PASSES.
